topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the open-plan office hummed with a grating, irritating monotony. Sarah stared at the presentation slides flickering on the main screen, her jaw tightening until her molars ached. Every word the CEO, Mr. Henderson, had used about "synergistic growth" sounded like a deliberate insult. She had seen the internal memos, the hushed, furtive conversations in the breakroom—the whispers that the company wasn't just expanding, it was being packaged up. A cold knot began to form in her stomach, spreading outwards with a sudden, unwelcome urgency. She slammed her laptop shut, the sharp click echoing disproportionately loud in the quiet space. "This is preposterous," she muttered to the empty cubicle, running a hand over her tired face. "They're selling us off piece by piece, and nobody even bothers to tell the people who actually build this place."

[story 2]

Mark leaned back in his ergonomic chair, the leather groaning under his weight as he meticulously cross-referenced the quarterly reports with the leaked acquisition documents. His knuckles were white where he gripped the edge of the desk. A slow, simmering heat began to rise in his chest, making his breathing shallow. He felt a profound sense of betrayal, a deep-seated resentment toward the executives who had built their empire only to treat their loyal workforce as disposable assets. He pushed the documents away, the paper rustling loudly. "Unbelievable," he breathed out, the sound raspy. He stood up abruptly, pacing the length of his workstation, his movements jerky and sharp. He needed to know the truth, to confront the deception head-on, to see the arrogance of it all laid bare.

[story 3]

From the periphery of the conference room, David watched the negotiation team finalizing the terms. He felt a distinct pressure building behind his eyes, a hot, prickly sensation that made his skin prickle. He had spent the last six months pouring his expertise into this firm, believing in its mission, its stability. Now, watching them discuss the liquidation of key divisions, a wave of sheer outrage washed over him. He stood up, his chair scraping back across the polished floor. He walked towards the glass wall overlooking the city, his fists clenched so tightly his nails bit into his palms. "They think they can just sweep us aside?" he challenged the silence of the room, his voice low but vibrating with contained fury. "This isn't a business transaction; it's an eviction."
